content.text: , WebSockets are useful when you need true bidirectional control during execution, such as interrupting, steering, approving steps, or injecting new context while the agent is still running. But for Claude specifically, Anthropic’s native model streaming and Managed Agents streaming are based on
, so the strongest production architecture is usually not “Claude over WebSocket directly,” but
Claude SSE on the backend, WebSocket between your app server and the browser when the UI needs live bidirectional agent control
Anthropic’s streaming docs state that Messages API streaming uses
with SSE, and the event flow includes

and error events. Anthropic’s Managed Agents overview likewise says Claude Managed Agents stream back results through SSE and are intended for long-running, stateful sessions that can be steered or interrupted over time. So if the question is “does Claude itself expose WebSocket-native streaming?”, the practical answer is no: the Claude-facing side is SSE-oriented, while your product can still choose WebSocket as the

WebSockets are most justified when the user must talk back to the running agent before it finishes. Examples include canceling a tool run, approving a risky action, adding clarifications mid-flight, watching collaborative updates across multiple clients, or resuming a long-lived agent session across tabs with synchronized state. If your UI only needs one-way token streaming, SSE is usually simpler and more aligned with Claude’s own APIs.
The strongest production pattern is usually a
Durable task/session store for state, replay, resumability, and reconnect recovery.
This design lets your UI benefit from bidirectional realtime control while keeping your Claude integration aligned with the official streaming model.

A pure WebSocket design sounds clean, but Claude does not natively require or benefit from WebSocket on the model side for ordinary token delivery. Anthropic’s own APIs already define SSE event semantics, including tool-use deltas, thinking deltas, pings, and resumability guidance, so your backend would still need to translate those concepts somehow. In most cases, WebSocket should be your
For long-running agents, do not stream only text. Instead, define a typed event protocol over your WebSocket such as:
This mirrors Claude’s real event-driven behavior much better than a single “assistant is typing” concept.

Anthropic’s streaming docs explain that Claude emits text deltas, tool-use input JSON deltas, thinking deltas, message-level stop reasons, and stream errors. Your backend should translate those low-level SSE events into higher-level UI events that are meaningful to the frontend, such as “Searching docs,” “Running tool,” or “Waiting for approval,” instead of exposing raw provider stream shapes directly to React components. This translation layer is one of the main reasons to introduce a realtime gateway server in the first place.

Anthropic documents Managed Agents as best for tasks that run for minutes or hours, with stateful sessions, persistent history, sandbox state, and mid-execution steering or interruption. That makes Managed Agents particularly well matched to a WebSocket-based frontend shell, because the browser can hold a durable conversation with

session server while the actual agent runs asynchronously in Anthropic-managed infrastructure. In that model, the WebSocket is really a control and observability channel around a persistent agent session.
A solid session model for long-running agent UIs is:
One append-only event log per session.
One active execution state snapshot for fast reconnect.
One or more subscribed clients over WebSocket for live updates.
This allows page refresh, tab handoff, and mobile reconnection without losing the agent’s place.

Long-running agent UIs fail if reconnect logic is weak. Cloudflare’s agent guidance in the results highlights resumable streaming and synchronization across disconnects, while broader production guidance emphasizes replayable event logs and graceful reconnection behavior. The right pattern is: when the client reconnects, it sends the last seen event sequence number, and the server replays missed events before resuming live push.

Anthropic’s streaming docs note that streaming can be interrupted by network issues and that recovery strategies exist, especially for text continuation in earlier and later model families. But the browser should not have to manage raw provider resume logic directly for a long-running agent session. Instead, your backend should absorb Claude-side interruptions and maintain a stable frontend event stream so the UI reconnects to
, not to a fragile provider stream boundary.
A practical WebSocket protocol should be command-based in one direction and event-based in the other. Good client-to-server messages often include:
Good server-to-client events are the agent progress events described earlier.
In React or Next.js, treat the WebSocket as an
, not as your only source of truth. A good frontend state shape usually has:
for outputs such as code, files, or charts.
This separation prevents UI chaos when agent progress becomes richer than plain text.

In a Next.js app, the WebSocket server often lives outside App Router route handlers, for example in a dedicated Node server, a managed realtime platform, or infrastructure that supports long-lived sockets cleanly. App Router remains useful for initial page load, session bootstrap, REST fallbacks, and artifact fetching, while the WebSocket channel handles live agent execution state. This is often cleaner than forcing every long-lived connection pattern through standard request-response primitives.
A good client abstraction is a custom hook such as

that manages connection state, event replay, and command sending. Its responsibilities usually include opening the socket, subscribing to the session, updating local stores from incoming events, exposing commands like
This is much more durable than treating everything as raw text.

WebSockets are especially valuable when the agent must pause and wait for user approval. Anthropic’s tool-use model already involves a stepwise loop between model output and tool execution, so adding human approval is a natural extension of that control surface. In the UI, approval should appear as a blocking but resumable state, where the agent timeline pauses, an approval card is shown, and the user can approve, reject, or edit the plan and send it back over the socket.
Anthropic Managed Agents explicitly support steering or interrupting by sending additional user events mid-execution. That maps naturally to WebSockets because the browser can issue an
command immediately without waiting for the current stream to finish. This is one of the clearest cases where WebSockets genuinely improve UX over pure SSE.

Production realtime UIs need backpressure handling. If Claude or your tools emit rapid event bursts, the server should batch or throttle updates before pushing them to the browser, and the React client should coalesce frequent deltas rather than re-rendering on every microscopic event. Without this, long-running agent sessions can feel slower precisely because they are “too realtime.”

events, and long-lived systems generally need heartbeats to detect dead connections. Your WebSocket layer should similarly implement ping/pong or periodic liveness timestamps so the client can distinguish “agent is thinking slowly” from “the connection is dead.” That distinction matters a lot during multi-minute runs.
Cancellation should be modeled explicitly, not as a dropped socket. A user clicking Stop should send a command like
, the server should acknowledge that the interruption request was accepted, and the UI should transition through states like
depending on what the backend confirms. This avoids ambiguous UX where users do not know whether the agent actually stopped.
Async fallback for very long work

One of the most important caveats from the search results is that for truly long-running background workloads, such as overnight jobs or processing huge corpora, neither WebSocket nor SSE should be the sole reliability mechanism. In those cases, a queue plus durable persistence is the better backbone, with WebSocket used only for live status when a client happens to be connected. In other words, use WebSocket for interactivity, not as your only job-control system.

Because WebSockets are long-lived and stateful, authorization must be checked both at connection time and at session-subscription time. Each incoming command should be validated against the current user’s rights for the

, and outbound events should be filtered so only authorized clients receive them. This matters even more for agent sessions because they may expose tool outputs, files, or sensitive intermediate state.
when the experience is mostly one-way token or progress streaming. Use
when users need to interrupt, guide, approve, collaborate, or reconnect into a live agent session. Use
, optionally with WebSocket on top, when the work may outlive any single browser session by a large margin.

A strong concise explanation is: “Claude itself streams over SSE, not WebSocket, so for long-running agents I usually put WebSocket only on the browser-to-app boundary. My app server consumes Claude SSE or Managed Agents events, translates them into a typed session event protocol, persists them to an event log, and pushes them to subscribed clients over WebSocket. That gives me bidirectional controls like interrupt, approval, and mid-run steering, while preserving reconnect, replay, and durable execution for multi-minute agent sessions.”
